item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The sheer upsetting horror of it was enough for me. And who the fuck piled them up like that!?

Honestly, this didn't really need the note at the end, but it was enjoyable either way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: God dammit, you literally made the "farm in upstate New York" that pets get "sent" to. Bravo! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: seems like end of death to me.

item.title: Re: I'm sorry to ask my little noob question :(
item.content:encoded: I don’t see any collapsibles. It appears that all addenda in this article are visible by default.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was *definitely* unprepared for the log.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's been a time since a final note gutted me like this one did. Jesus. Well done, I hate it, I upvoted. This one is going to linger in my mind something awful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just posted a little dialogue update, hopefully it maintains tone a little better. As for the rest, well, I can't exactly do all that much more to make you feel bad for the animals so ill just take the L on that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Similar to what others have said before, the dialogue, when read, feels off to me. Like when they see actual zombies, they're startled, but for the most of it, up until one of them throws up, they seemed chilled—that's weird to me.

Also, going inside the article itself, the reveal of the animals decaying didn't do anything for me. It was gross, buts that about it, there's nothing else to it other than the note at the end. The note had some neat implications—human consciousness of lying so much made their "farm" real and this is where some of those animals go. But that alone is not enough for me to enjoy an article, especially since that aspect wasn't really the focus of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ow, my feelings.

It's perfect. Squickly and poignant and about as concise as one can make it. I kinda wish you had gone with humorous instead because now I'm ready to have sad cries about Liam missing his Peaches so dearly, and his coward parents for not just telling him that Peaches was gone… Goddammit it's just so sad.

Also since the animals don't succumb to their injuries, it makes me wonder if cremation is actually the end for them or if they stick around with the ashes… It's absolutely bizarre to consider but it also gives me fridge-logic creeps.

Anyway, +1, obviously.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeahh that was a recent addition to address the earlier criticisms. Glad it helped!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Jeeeesus christ, that's sad. That's just sad.

+1 for making me sad.

Also, for the record, the containment procedures were pretty good. I read that the healthy instances were to be contained but ones in decay to be burned was a moment of "Wait, what?" I honestly liked its minimalist style.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That bit of it came from personal experience with how cops will handle things like roadkill. I've seen that for the most part, they will put the animals down themselves and call animal control for removal. I can see that being a bit of a confounding detail though. I'll be combing through the log later to see what kinks I can iron out.

E: Dialogue has been fixed up according to criticism. Hope it's better now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think you've built a good foundation for the two cops for sure, but a little more flavor wouldn't hurt.

I think in this situation you could have a quick, effective cascade of breaking tone. First they're chummy and "lets get this done" sort of informal, but once they see the decrepid state of the home and smell the blood, that tone breaks way to a more formal, defensive tension of "cop mode". Declaring they're entering, stating they have a warrant, pulling guns, etc. Then, after trying to put down the dog and being confronted by the anomaly, that tone breaks as well, and they sink into revulsion and fear.

Also, if these are supposed to be simple townie cops, the idea that the dialogue would close with "we've got a find a way to put these things down" seems… odd. Like, if they aren't Foundation, they should be far more disturbed and confused by these events. Solution seeking when confronted with the anomalous is more a Foundation response.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: glad someone took that idea at least :P +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the conprocs suffer from the fact that I wrote them before I really decided how I wanted to end this thing and then I never revisited them. I'm gonna take a look and see what I can do regarding how we deal with the animals.

As for the dialogue, I'll play around with it, but my intentions were that these were just some beater small town cops fucking around and not taking this all that seriously until the situation got real messy. I'll see about giving them more personality than just a northeast accent though, because that's a mega valid complaint.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First and foremost, +1 for the twist. Excellent concept, well delivered, didn't overstay it's welcome. I do have to agree with Roget and Rounder on this though.

The containment procedures do very little to lead the mystery, and don't really function. If we haven't figured out how to kill these animals, humanely or otherwise, then this is a much bigger issue warranting greater response. As it stands, this feels like "toss them in a pen". Even the addition of "until a solution can be devised" would have made this feel more complete. A unique method of eliminating these animals using anomalous, or just Foundation level technology would have closed the loop as well without subtracting from the story.

The dialogue feels stilted, unprofessional, and lacks a cohesive tone. You also go a bit too hard on the accent with "them" and whatnot. The impact of "the dog can't die" would have had much more weight if these agents had started off by-the-books, then having that broken by the horror of the situation. The good part is the actual skeleton is strong: the shock of the dog not dying after multiple gunshots is well executed and paced.

It's still a +1 for sure, but it needs to be tightened. Good work McB.

item.title: Re: I'm sorry to ask my little noob question :(
item.content:encoded: How do you mean?

item.title: I'm sorry to ask my little noob question :(
item.content:encoded: Im sorry to ask, but I'm new on this. How do you make like, addendum files and interview files visible??

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found the dialogue in the log to be stiff as a board, which is less than great considering the log is the meat of the article. The note at the end is fine as notes at the end go, but it didn't really progress my understanding of the anomaly or reveal new information to me.
Oh no

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is what I was actually expecting to happen haha

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Spoilers
- Title for hiding text

Don't know if it would be funny or horrifying if a Researcher found that one of the dog corpses was wearing the collar of his childhood pet dog, that his mom told him "left to go live on the farm."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is such a simple idea but it's executed so well, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I may revisit those conprocs tomorrow, but I rather like the description being vague because it leaves the reader totally unprepared for the log. Glad you liked it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The containment procedures are a total waste, you could have done a lot more to set up the article. As it stands they're rather perfunctory and are basically a waste of space. The description is acceptable but doesn't really do anything amazing beyond setting up the log. The log itself and the final note are visceral and gripping by the end. The note-at-the-end cliche is about as worn out as cliche's get but I'll be damned if it doesn't work here. +1 for pulling that off. The brevity makes it work overall, I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So long, it was so long ago,
but I've still got the blues for you.

Gary Moore ~ Still Got the Blues

This was my attempt at Bright's Challenge. I think it turned out pretty alright. Feel free to fix any typos and stuff. Staring at the wikidot edit box for so long tends to make me glaze over some stuff.

Click Here for more stuff by me!
